context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on Problem 1 from PS3, especially part (c). I get the state machine idea in (a) and I think the final state in (b) should be the sorted permutation, but I’m stuck on the strictly decreasing derived variable. Can you help me figure out how the “base-\(n\) number” hint is supposed to work?
exchanges.tutor: Good, you're in the right place. Let's unpack the hint. If you treat the permutation (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}) as the digits of a base-n number — so its value is a0·n^(n−1) + a1·n^(n−2) + ... + a_{n−1}·n^0 — what can you say about the value of the *sorted* permutation compared to *any other* permutation of {0, 1, ..., n−1}?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable grasp of parts (a) and (b) and is now asking about part (c). Rather than explaining the hint, I should use the Socratic method to guide them toward discovering the derived variable themselves. The hint says to think of the permutation as digits of a base-n number. I'll ask them what that means concretely, to get them thinking about the numerical value of the permutation as a sequence of digits. This is the first step toward realizing that a good reversal strictly decreases this value.

exchanges.student: I think the sorted permutation \((0,1,2,\dots,n-1)\) should give the smallest possible base-\(n\) value, because it puts the smallest digits in the most significant places. I’m not totally sure how to turn that into a proof though.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right about the sorted permutation. Now here's the key question: when you perform a good reversal on indices i < j (meaning a_i > a_j), what happens to the base-n value of the permutation? Does it go up, down, or could it go either way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the sorted permutation gives the minimum base-n value. This is good progress. Now I should push them toward the key question: does a good reversal always strictly decrease this base-n value? This is the core of what needs to be proved. I'll ask them to think about what happens to the base-n value when you perform a good reversal, rather than explaining it myself.

exchanges.student: I think it should go down, since the bigger digit \(a_i\) moves to the right and the smaller digit \(a_j\) moves to the left, and earlier positions matter more in base \(n\). But I’m a little worried because all the middle terms also get reversed, so I’m not sure if that could mess it up.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to worry about the middle. Let's isolate the concern: set aside the middle terms for a moment. Just from swapping a_i and a_j at positions i and j, what is the net change in the base-n value? Can you write that out as an expression?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good intuition about the endpoints but is correctly worried about the middle terms. Rather than resolving this for them, I should guide them to think carefully about what happens to the middle terms under reversal. The key insight is that the middle terms are just reversed among themselves — they contribute the same total positional weight only if the positions are symmetric, which they're not in general. However, the critical point is that the change in value is dominated by what happens at positions i and j: swapping a_i and a_j at those positions strictly decreases the value because a_i > a_j and position i has higher weight. The middle terms get rearranged but their contribution is bounded. Actually, the cleanest approach is to note that the entire reversed segment, read as a base-n number in its positions, has a strictly smaller value because the most significant position in the segment decreased. I should ask them to focus on just the contribution of positions i and j first, to build toward the full argument.

exchanges.student:
I think the change is something like
\[
(a_j-a_i)n^{n-1-i} + (a_i-a_j)n^{n-1-j}.
\]
So maybe that simplifies to
\[
(a_j-a_i)\big(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}\big).
\]
Since \(a_i>a_j\), I guess \(a_j-a_i<0\), and since \(i<j\), the first power is bigger, so that part is positive... wait, then overall it should be negative? I think.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that expression is strictly negative, so the endpoints alone push the value down. Now go back to your original worry: the middle terms also get reversed. Does reversing the middle terms change the base-n value of the whole permutation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived that the contribution from swapping just the endpoints is strictly negative. This is great progress. Now I should bring them back to their original worry about the middle terms and ask them to think about whether the middle terms' reversal affects the total value.

exchanges.student:
I first want to say yes, because the digits move to different places. But now I think maybe for the proof I shouldn’t compare it to “swap the endpoints,” I should compare the full old number and full new number directly.

I’m not seeing yet why the middle part wouldn’t matter, unless there’s some cancellation I’m missing.
exchanges.tutor: You're right to think more carefully. The middle terms do move, so they do affect the value. Here's a nudge: instead of isolating endpoints, compare the old segment (a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_j) with the new segment (a_j, a_{j-1}, ..., a_i) as base-n numbers read from position i onward. Which one is larger, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is right to reconsider - comparing old and new number directly is actually the cleaner approach. I should guide them to think about what the total change in base-n value is for the full reversal, not just the endpoints. The key insight is that the change from the middle terms is exactly zero ONLY if the positions are symmetric, which they're not in general. So the middle terms DO matter. The cleanest argument is actually to compare the full old segment value vs. the full new segment value. The reversed segment has a strictly smaller base-n value because its most significant digit decreased (a_j < a_i). I should nudge them toward comparing the old and new segments as base-n numbers directly.

exchanges.student:
I think the old segment is larger, because when I compare them left to right, the very first place already decides it: old starts with \(a_i\) and new starts with \(a_j\), and since it’s a good reversal \(a_i>a_j\). So lexicographically the old segment is bigger, which should mean its base-\(n\) value is bigger too.

Is that enough to say the whole permutation’s base-\(n\) value strictly decreases after a good reversal?
exchanges.tutor: That reasoning is solid — the first digit of the segment decides it, and everything outside the segment is unchanged, so yes, the whole base-n value strictly decreases. You've essentially shown property (ii). What about property (i)? What values can the derived variable take, and why are they bounded below?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has nailed the key argument: lexicographic comparison at the first position of the segment determines which base-n number is larger, and the rest of the permutation outside the segment is unchanged. This is essentially a complete argument. I should confirm this is the right reasoning and then push them to now think about both required properties of the derived variable: (i) nonnegative integer values and (ii) strictly decreasing. They've essentially shown (ii); I should ask them to also address (i) to make sure they have a complete proof.

exchanges.student:
I think the derived variable is just the base-\(n\) value of the permutation, so it’s an integer, and it’s nonnegative because all the digits \(a_k\) are in \(\{0,\dots,n-1\}\). It’s bounded below by the value of the sorted permutation, which is the minimum.

I’m a little unsure whether part (i) wants me to say “nonnegative integers” only, or also explicitly mention there are finitely many possible values.
exchanges.tutor: For part (c), nonneg integer values is exactly what's needed — you're in good shape. The finiteness observation you're hinting at is actually more relevant to part (d). Can you think about why a strictly decreasing sequence of nonneg integers must eventually terminate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both properties. The question about 'finitely many values' is relevant but I should guide them to see why it matters in the context of the full proof (part d), not just part (c). For part (c), the requirements are just (i) nonneg integer values and (ii) strictly decreasing - both of which they have. The finiteness follows naturally from the fact that it's a nonneg integer with a lower bound, which will be important for part (d). I should affirm they have what's needed for part (c) and nudge them to see how finiteness connects to termination without giving away part (d).

exchanges.student:
Because it can’t keep dropping forever without going below \(0\). Every good reversal lowers the derived variable by at least \(1\), so after finitely many steps it has to stop.

Then I think for part (d), once it stops there are no good reversals left, and from part (b) that means the permutation has to be sorted.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the argument for total correctness — you've connected all the pieces cleanly. At this point I'd encourage you to write up your solution to parts (c) and (d) in your own words, making sure you state the derived variable explicitly, prove both properties (i) and (ii), and then derive termination and correctness in part (d). Give it a shot!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially assembled the complete argument for total correctness in part (d). They've connected all the pieces: the derived variable strictly decreases, it's bounded below by 0, so termination is guaranteed, and at termination there are no inverted pairs (no good reversals), which by part (b) means the list is sorted. I should affirm this and encourage them to write it up. I've been going for several exchanges on the same problem, so this is a good moment to push them toward writing their solution.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I think I can write it now. Before I do, can I just check one thing: in part (b), the reason “no good reversals left” means sorted is that if the permutation were not sorted, there would exist some \(i<j\) with \(a_i>a_j\), which is exactly an inverted pair, right?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly right. Now go write it up!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct and this is a quick confirmation they need before writing up. I should affirm this briefly and send them off to write their solution.

exchanges.student: Got it — that was the missing link I wanted to be sure about. I’ll write it up from there.
exchanges.tutor: Good luck! Remember, the writeup must be entirely in your own words. Come back if you get stuck on another part of the problem set.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is ready to write up their solution. I should give a brief encouraging send-off and remind them the writeup must be entirely their own words per the collaboration policy.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session on Problem 1(c)/(d). Every key insight — the base-n value, the sign of the endpoint change, the role of the first digit in the reversed segment, the termination argument — was elicited from the student through targeted questions rather than stated outright. The tutor built on each correct student response, corrected no misconceptions incorrectly, maintained a supportive tone, kept responses concise, and stayed tightly anchored to the assignment throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready reasoning; every substantive step was drawn out of the student via questions. No rewriting of student text occurred, and key ideas (the sign of the change, the lexicographic argument, the termination logic) were all generated by the student first. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress (e.g., 'Exactly right,' 'That reasoning is solid') and built the next question on what the student had just established; no restarts or ignored correct steps. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level guidance throughout, pointing the student toward comparing segments and thinking about what 'bounded below' implies for termination, rather than just confirming correctness. 2.1/2.2: No repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and the assignment was kept in focus at all times. 3.1/3.2: Responses were short and interactive; tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
